claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex, who's 13, and Blake, who's 15. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (13) and Blake (15).

Now, to assess your eligibility, I need to ask:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and Blake:

**Do Alex and Blake live with you, or if they don't live with you, are you paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me at home. Blake doesn't live with me, but I do contribute towards their upkeep - I help out with things like clothes and other expenses they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. To better understand Blake's situation:

**How much do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep each month (or week), and do you know the current Child Benefit rates to compare whether your contribution is at least equal to what Child Benefit would pay?**

(For reference, the current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children, though I'll need to verify if your contributions meet the threshold.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute about £26 a week towards Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information. Now I need to ask:

**Is Blake (who is 15) currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now let me ask about Alex:

**Is Alex (who is 13) currently in education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions:

**Do either Alex or Blake work 24 hours or more per week, or are they claiming any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work that much, and they don't claim any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period (more than 8-12 weeks)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in care, but it's only been for about a month or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both children.

**For Alex (age 13):**
You're eligible because Alex lives with you, is under 16, is in school, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (age 15):**
You're eligible because:
- Although Blake doesn't live with you, you contribute £26 per week towards their upkeep, which meets the requirement (it exceeds the Child Benefit rates)
- Blake is still under 16, so the education/training requirement doesn't apply yet (that only comes into effect when children turn 16)
- Blake has only been in care for about a month, which is less than the 8-week threshold that would affect your Child Benefit payments

**Important note about Blake's situation:**
If Blake remains in care for more than 8 weeks total, your Child Benefit payments for Blake might be affected unless Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home. You should keep the Child Benefit Office informed of any changes to Blake's circumstances.

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for both children. If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), lives with you, is in school, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Alex does not work 24+ hours per week, does not claim benefits in their own right, and is not in hospital or care. You meet all the eligibility criteria for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) and does not live with you, but you contribute £26 per week towards their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 for an eldest child or £16.95 for an additional child. This means you are paying at least as much as Child Benefit towards Blake's care. Although Blake is not currently in approved education or training, at age 15 (under 16), this is not a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility - the education/training requirement only applies to children aged 16-19. Blake has been in care for about a month, which is less than the 8-week threshold that would affect payments. No one else is claiming for Blake, Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, and doesn't claim benefits in their own right. You meet the eligibility criteria for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has two children. For Alex (13), they meet the standard eligibility criteria as Alex lives with them and is under 16. For Blake (15), although Blake doesn't live with them and is in care, the claimant contributes £26 per week towards Blake's upkeep (meeting the contribution requirement) and Blake has only been in care for about a month (less than the 8-week threshold). Both children are under 16, so education/training requirements don't apply yet. No one else is claiming for either child. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children.

Blake has been in care for about a month, which is less than the 8-week threshold that would affect payments. No one else is claiming for Blake, Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, and doesn't claim benefits in their own right. You meet the eligibility criteria for Blake.

Both children are under 16, so education/training requirements don't apply yet. No one else is claiming for either child. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children.
